speaker: interviewer
text: How is this one? Today's date is December the 21st, 1995. My name is Linda Davidson. And I am interviewing the survivor, Paula Mintz, nee Herling. The interview is taking place in Toronto, Canada and is being conducted in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Today's date is December the 21st, 1995. My name is Linda Davidson. And I'm conducting an interview with the survivor, Paula Mintz, nee Herling. The interview is being conducted in English and is taking place in Toronto, Canada.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please tell us your name.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Paula Mintz.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: P-A-U-L-A Mintz, M-I-N-T-Z.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Is-- my maiden name is Herling, H-E-R-L-I-N-G.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were born, were you given a different name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was Pesa Herling.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how do you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: P-E-S-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you born, Paula?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born the 3rd of October, 1919.

speaker: interviewee
text: And where were you born? I was born in Suchedniov near Kielce. How do you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Suchedniov? S-U-S-H-- C-H, excuse me, E-D-N-I-O-V.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what country is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Poland. And this is [POLISH] or this is like region Kielce.

speaker: interviewer
text: Paula, we'd like to learn about your family and your pre-war life. What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Itzhak Mayer Herling.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of man was he? What do you remember of him?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have very, very great memories of my father. He was nicknamed the good Itzhak because he was doing so many good things to kids. They loved him, Gentile and Jewish people. And he had a soul of gold. He did help lots of people. And he was a very honest man, religious, but not fanatic. He even kissed the ladies' hands. Because we had all kinds of people in our business where my father belonged. And he was very generous. People knew him. He was a very honest and humble man. I have great memories of him. Unfortunately, he died in the Treblinka. So my heart is bleeding for that. But I can't help it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that he was in contact with many people in business.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he do for a living to support the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a clerk and expediter in a large iron factory. And he also, later on, we had our own little business taking from-- we sold from a company, clay for porcelain. And we send it with large-- from the large wagons from the-- with the train.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we had this company. And they liked my father very much. And he gave them the clay. He had clay mines.

speaker: interviewee
text: He took the claims for free and gave it-- that's why we had it. And my father made a living out of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was your father born?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was born in Suchedniov, the same.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your father's family has been in Suchedniov--

speaker: interviewee
text: Suchedniov for a long time, maybe for hundreds of years. We didn't know any other place. This was our birthplace. I was born and lived in the same place all my life and all my family. We didn't move at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your mother. What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name was Hannah Rivka.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Stern. Hannah Rivka Stern. When she married my father, my father was a widower with two children, one son and a daughter. But they didn't live with us. They lived-- the daughter lived with my father's mother. And the son was married, with three children. And we were at home, eight children of us from the same mother and father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was your mother born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Radom.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you happen to know how they met?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Never asked them that.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were telling me how many brothers and sisters you had.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes I had, with me, six daughters-- six daughters and two brothers.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of family life can you tell us about? What kind of atmosphere in the home?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were a very close family. Each of us, when we grew up did something else. I, for instance, was working in a lumber mill. My older sisters were tutoring children from school. And one was doing embroidery for all kinds of people. And the other was younger than I am. One was apprenticing in the-- by a good seamstress. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the other was still at home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother, was she a housewife before?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was only a housewife, and a good one too. I remember, she was getting up in the mornings, very early in the morning. And she prepared us all for school. We went off to school, looking after us very well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the schooling in Suchedniov. What kind of schools did you attend there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I attended public school, seven classes. And then I was apprenticeship-- in an apprenticeship in the lumber yard, for a year or so. They taught me how to do it. And then I worked full time, till the war broke out, as a bookkeeper.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to go back a little bit to your school years. Was there a name by which the school was known, the public school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was only one school, one public school where I attended.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the language of instruction at the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: The language was Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your favorite subjects at school, Paula?

speaker: interviewee
text: My favorite subject in school was grammatic and geography.

speaker: interviewer
text: At school, were you friendly with the non-Jewish students as well as Jewish students?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, very friendly, because we lived very close together. They were my friends from, from childhood. So we came to each other. But some of them were nasty and did some special bad things to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: They chased us around. They, they spoke bad, bad language to us. And they were very, very unhappy-- un, un-- they weren't nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did that feel like?

speaker: interviewee
text: They felt that this is-- we did bad things to them, the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did that make you feel like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It made feel me very bad. I felt very bad. Sometimes I came home from school crying. But my mother, [POLISH] she says, don't take care of what they say. You are what you are. And you are, you should be proud of yourself. So, we go and went on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware whether your father experienced any anti-Jewish feeling at his work in the same manner?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't know about that. He wasn't because he was very-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he was-- everybody liked him. He was a good man.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he wasn't experiencing anything bad while he was working. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's all that I know. I never heard about any occurrences about this. I can say that.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the situation wasn't great. Sometimes at home, we were-- my father was out, and somebody came in. And they did some things wrong.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me more.

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't recall. But my mother told me that once she was chased after-- with a buggy and a horse. And she fell down. That's what I remember once happened, wintertime. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Growing up as a young girl in Suchedniov, what were your hobbies? What were your dreams when you looked towards the future?

speaker: interviewee
text: My hobbies when I was in the all the classes, like five, six, seven, I was very inclined with sports. So I did--

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of sports?

speaker: interviewee
text: All kinds of sports. I was, I was loving to go out hikes, I liked to go for walks. And I played volleyball. I played other balls. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was a very alive girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: During summer vacation, what would you and your family do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just we stayed home. We couldn't afford a summer vacation to go out. And besides, nobody did that at home. Because our place was like a summer vacation. People came to see-- to our city for vacations. Because it was a very nice little city, with-- good for the sick people. So they came from all over. There were nice woods and [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: So they came to our city. And we have company with them. We played with them together. And it was pretty good summertime. Did you have a large family in Suchedniov, your mother's family, your father's family?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father-- mother's family, nobody was there. Only my father's family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was your mother's family?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Radom. My father-- my mother had two sisters in Radom and with families.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you visit one another often?

speaker: interviewee
text: Quite often I went to Radom. They came to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far away was Radom from Suchedniov?

speaker: interviewee
text: About 300, 400 miles. I don't know exactly.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how would the journey be made?

speaker: interviewee
text: By train. We went by train, yes. And they came to us. And I was quite a few times there by myself in Radom.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you say that your father's family were in Suchedniov?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Suchedniov.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you get together and celebrate Shabbat, Yom [? Tovim ?] together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes, sometimes, not very often, because we all had large families. And there weren't any such big houses, like we have here. We lived very-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in two, two rooms, two or three, three rooms. And we didn't have room for making big parties like that. So everybody celebrated by themselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: Paula, tell me about Shabbat in your home. How was that celebrated?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a great holiday. My father went to shul. And my mother prepared her nice food. And we all were together, nobody missed at the table. It wasn't supposed to be anybody missing. We sat down together. We sang songs after the dinner. It was very traditional.

speaker: interviewer
text: If I was a guest in your home, what kind of food would I be eating there?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] Traditional Jewish food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me more. Tell me about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Gefilte fish, or any other fish, soup with noodles, some good meat, and some dessert. And then we had some fruit, fresh fruits. And then we went out to play. We came home. We had a snack, and again, out to play. And that's how it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were relations between the Jewish people and the non-Jewish people in Suchedniov like?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Suchedniov, the anti-Semitism was evident. But where I lived, we lived very close, because we lived in a house where it belonged to Gentiles. And they had big families. And we have-- we were very close with each other. So I did not feel that anti-Semitism so great. But otherwise, I heard of other bad incidents with, with the Gentiles and Jews. But I didn't take notice of that then, because we couldn't do differently. We had to be in good terms with each other. They came to our house, we came to them. And they liked us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this ever discussed with your parents, the relationship between Jews and non-Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they didn't mind our Gentile friends coming in the house. They were very close with it-- even with my parents. They liked us. So my mother didn't have anything against it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Different times of the year, was there a different reaction towards the Jews from the non-Jewish people?

speaker: interviewee
text: During the Christmas holidays it was better. But when they had the heralds, the twelve days of the Israel days and they were clothed differently. So they chased after us. When-- in the evenings, that is. So I didn't go out at all, these holidays.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they were coming to us and chasing us and scaring us very badly. And we were young, we didn't understand. So we were running away from them, and they were chasing us. And so we were very afraid. And during the Easter holidays, they weren't so, so sympathetic with the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they always claimed that the Jews took some blood for the matzah what we had. And this was a very wrong idea what they had. Because this was not true. Your matzahs have to be straight made from water and flour, not anything else, not even salt, nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how could that be that we used to the blood from them? This was a legend from years and years, centuries ago. And they kept on believing this. How would this anti-Jewish feeling be expressed?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't speak to us too much. They were a little further away from us. And I don't remember any bad incidents about it but we could see the difference between it. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It's always-- but all in all, in our little town, it wasn't so bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: How big was the Jewish community in Suchedniov?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know exactly. But I would think there were about 250 to 300 families in the whole little city.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this a close community?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you describe the majority of Jewish workers in Suchedniov? What kind of business did they pursue?

speaker: interviewee
text: There wasn't too much work for the Jewish people. A lot of people had some little business. A lot of people lived in very great poverty, the Jewish people. And some people were working in lumber mills or there was another mill. Some people did work-- were apprentices. And there were a lot of cobblers and seamstresses. This is, this was their profession.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a so-called Jewish area in Suchedniov?

speaker: interviewee
text: A Jewish area?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the Jews live--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah?

speaker: interviewer
text: --in a particular area together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that was on the, in shtetl. It called the shtetl. And then it was, because the, the street was called [POLISH]. And there was, the Jews were located.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the shuls, the Jewish facilities that existed in Suchedniov.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had one, one shul. There were few stiebel, where Jews came to daven. But my father belonged to the shul. And he was a member there. And that's all what it was, there wasn't too much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you remember the name of the rabbi?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wouldn't, no. I don't remember. But he had also a big family. And the, his kids were friends even with my brother. They were friendly people. And they belonged-- they didn't belong sometimes to the shul. They belonged to other organizations.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language did you speak at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: At home we spoke Yiddish. But sometimes Polish, too, between us, between the sisters. But mostly Yiddish. And we knew the Polish language just the same as our-- my Polish girlfriends. Exactly, or maybe better. Because I was very good in grammar and so were my-- we were admired for that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What other Jewish institutions were there? Was there mikvah or a Jewish library or anything like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they had Zionist organizations, Mizrahi organizations. I belonged to-- I went to [NON-ENGLISH], a Jewish school for girls only. And that taught me a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that after public school?

speaker: interviewee
text: After public school, in the evenings. Yeah, every evening we went there. We performed some nice plays for each holiday, for, for Purim, for Pesach, for every-- every holiday we had a nice play.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where would you do these plays?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where would you have these plays take place?

speaker: interviewee
text: These plays were taken in the school where we, where we went, in the [NON-ENGLISH] mostly. And the parents came to see. It was very nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did any Yiddish plays come to Suchedniov, to play before the Jewish community?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were some. We had one show, one movie show, that's all, where it took place in a big shop from the fire, fire department. That's where they came to show us the shows. A Jewish show, I don't remember. But if one wants-- wanted to show-- to see a Jewish show, they had to go to a bigger city, like to Kielce, to Radom.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far was Kielce from Suchedniov?

speaker: interviewee
text: About 250 kilometers or maybe less. I can't recall. I wasn't interested in this then.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you interested in, Paula?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was mostly interested in playing and in work. And I didn't think of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there Jewish or Yiddish newspapers available in Suchedniov?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I read Jewish newspapers. I read Jewish books.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you remember the names of any of these newspapers?

speaker: interviewee
text: I know one Jewish paper was 'The Moment.' One was [NON-ENGLISH]. And I even had a whole set of Sholem Aleichem's books, which I read.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that there were Zionist organizations.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was Zionism an issue? Were people considering immigration to Israel in the years before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, there were people who left to Palestine then. They belonged to kibbutzim. And by us were a few kibbutzim, where people were working in the, in the mill where I was working. They gave them jobs there. And in other places from the kibbutzim, they came to work as women to clean the houses. They worked very hard. And they have their own commune. And they waited to the-- after a period of time, they were going home. And they were waiting for a certificate. But sometimes they never got it. And the war broke out, and they went-- they perished.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that your sisters, some of them had been married before the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: My one sister was married, during the war, 1939. That was before the war, but in that year. And she had one child.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was a Jewish wedding like in Suchedniov?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very nice. It wasn't-- mostly it was outside the synagogue. And then the, the service was in the synagogue. But the canopy and everything was outside. And then we went in for a, for a full meal at home or we-- on a bigger, big hall. It was just like here sometimes. But it wasn't so elaborate like here. But people lived very nicely and very, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] very happy. Even so, it wasn't so elaborate and so rich like here. I didn't hear at home about so many divorces like I hear here. It was a good life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are there any incidents from your childhood that you would like to share with us now?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't have-- my childhood was very, very simple. So was my other sisters. We didn't live so as people live here, the children do here, no, very simple and plain. But we, we were very close to each other. And that meant a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you finished your schooling, what did you decide to do as far as work is concerned?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wanted to work. And so my father knew these people. And he worked for them too.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of their company? [NON-ENGLISH] and [NON-ENGLISH] Lumber Mill. And what did you do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: First, as I said, they taught me how to be a bookkeeper, how to do managers. And then I did the full job of bookkeeping-- prepared it for the accountant to come. He came from Kielce. And I was paying out the pay for the workers, paying out the pay for the, for the people who came with the big, big numbers. And I was very efficient. I never made mistakes. They did really like me. I couldn't even go out with another worker from, from that place and do, measure out a full load of, of lumber. I was very good at it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you see your future at that stage?

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw as future that I'll stay there till the time comes, and I'll get married and go off. Because I liked the job. My father didn't let us go anywhere else. He kept us together. So I didn't see that I will go somewhere else. And I enjoyed the job. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was-- [SIGHS] I liked it because it was not far from my house. And my, my father came sometimes over to, to that place too, to have some, some kind of business, business doings. So it was very good to see him there. And they thought that I do a good job. And they told him about me. And he had [NON-ENGLISH].
